summary:
The Achaean League (281–146 BC) was a confederation of Greek city-states in the northern Peloponnese that developed into the most powerful Greek political organization of the [[event:80|Hellenistic Period]], serving as the last significant attempt at Greek political unity before Roman conquest ended Greek independence.

The League initially united Achaean cities for mutual defense, but under the statesman Aratus of Sicyon (who joined Sicyon to the League in 251 BC and served as its leading figure for decades) it expanded to include most of the Peloponnese including Corinth. The League's constitution provided for a federal assembly, elected generals, and proportional representation — a genuinely federal political experiment that influenced the American Founders' thinking about federal republics. The League's foreign policy was complicated by the pressure of the [[event:1042|Antigonid Dynasty]]'s Macedon, requiring alternating alliance and opposition. After defeating Macedonia in the [[event:1065|Macedonian Wars]], Rome initially allowed Greek cities nominal freedom, but Roman interference in Greek affairs increasingly irritated the League. In 146 BC — the same year as the [[event:1059|Destruction of Carthage]] — Rome crushed the Achaean League's resistance and destroyed Corinth, imposing direct control over Greece.
